batchClaims.descriptionTemplate:
In the language \thislang, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \thislang supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \thislang by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\thislang} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \thislang does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

In the language \thislang, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \thislang supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \thislang by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\thislang} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\thislang} and \compilesquasi{\thislang}{\langref{DNNF}}, \thislang cannot support bounded conjunction.

There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\thislang} and \compilesquasi{\thislang}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \thislang cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\thislang} and \compilesquasi{\thislang}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, \thislang cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

\citet[Theorem 5]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations of functions $f$ and $g$ such that, measured in the size $s$ of the corresponding source representations, $f \lor g$ requires \langref{d-SDNNF} size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$. The unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations can be transformed into \thislang representations with only polynomial overhead, and every \thislang representation can be transformed into \langref{d-SDNNF} with only polynomial overhead. Thus polynomial-time bounded disjunction for \thislang would contradict the lower bound.

If \thislang supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \thislang circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \thislang, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

\citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \thislang supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\thislang} and \compilesquasi{\thislang}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

In the language \thislang, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \thislang supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \thislang by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\thislang} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \thislang does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

In the language \thislang, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \thislang supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \thislang by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\thislang} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

If \thislang supported FO, then the consistency of any \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$ could be tested in polynomial time: represent $\Sigma$ in \thislang, compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$, and check the resulting constant representation. This would decide \langref{CNF} consistency in polynomial time, implying P = NP \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \thislang would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \thislang representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\thislang} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}

languages.name: CNF
languages.definition: A \langref{CNF} is a conjunction of clauses $\bigwedge_{i=1}^mC_i$. A clause is a disjunction of literals $C_i=\bigvee_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \lor y \lor \lnot z)\land (x\lor \lnot y)\land (z\lor w)$.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{PI}}{\langref{CNF}}. If \langref{CNF} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{PI} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: EQ implies CO: $\mathsf{true}$ and $\mathsf{false}$ belong to every NNF subset. $\Sigma$ is inconsistent iff it is equivalent to $\mathsf{false}$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\EQ}.

SE.description: SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\SE}.

CE.description: CE implies CO: A formula $\Sigma$ is inconsistent iff $\Sigma \models \bot$, which is equivalent to $\Sigma$ entailing the empty clause. Clause entailment (CE) can test this directly. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CE}.

ME.description: ME implies CO: Model Enumeration requires outputting all models in time polynomial in the input size and number of models. In particular, it must determine whether any model exists (output the first model or halt with the empty set) in polynomial time, which is exactly the Consistency (CO) query. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\ME}.

CO.description: Consistency checking for \langref{CNF} is NP-complete by the Cook-Levin theorem, the classic foundational result for SAT \citep{Cook_1971,Levin_1973}.

IM.description: To check whether a consistent term $\alpha$ implies a \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$, it suffices to verify that each clause of $\Sigma$ shares a literal with $\alpha$. This can be done in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

VA.description: A \langref{CNF} representation is valid iff every clause in it is valid (i.e., contains a complementary pair of literals). This can be checked in linear time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

FO.description: If \langref{CNF} supported FO, then the consistency of any \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$ could be tested in polynomial time: represent $\Sigma$ in \langref{CNF}, compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$, and check the resulting constant representation. This would decide \langref{CNF} consistency in polynomial time, implying P = NP \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\ANDC}. Since Conjunction implies bounded conjunction: bounded conjunction is a special case of conjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under conjunction is also closed under bounded conjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\ANDBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CD}. \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\ORBC}. Since Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component). \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\SFO}.

NOT_C.description: Negation and conjunction imply disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \lor \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, conjoin, then negate the result. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\ORC}. Also, \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\ANDC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\NOTC}.

AND_C.description: Conjunction of \langref{CNF} representation is trivially another \langref{CNF} representation: simply take the union of all clauses \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: The disjunction of two \langref{CNF} representations $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one clause from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and valid clauses. The result is a \langref{CNF} of polynomial size \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent \langref{CNF} formula in polynomial time. Since specific \langref{DNF} formulas cannot be turned into equivalent \langref{CNF} formulas in polynomial space (\langref{DNF} $\not\leq$ \langref{CNF}), \langref{CNF} does not satisfy $\lor$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: CD is trivially satisfied: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants in a \langref{CNF} and removing valid clauses preserves the \langref{CNF} property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.name: cSDD

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\langref{SDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{SDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\langref{SDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{SDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \supportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\NOTC}. \supportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\CE}. Since Negation and clausal entailment imply implicant testing: term $\alpha$ implies $\Sigma$ iff $\lnot\Sigma$ entails clause $\lnot\alpha$. Negate $\Sigma$ via $\lnot$C, then test clausal entailment of $\lnot\alpha$ (a clause) via CE. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{cSDD}}. If \langref{cSDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{cSDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{cSDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{cSDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{cSDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{cSDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{cSDD}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{cSDD} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{cSDD} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{cSDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{cSDD} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{cSDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{cSDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{cSDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{cSDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{cSDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{cSDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

SFO.description: If \langref{cSDD} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{cSDD} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{cSDD}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\FO}.

NOT_C.description: Every \langref{cSDD} respects some vtree $T$. Apply the fixed-vtree negation procedure for \langfam{cSDD}{T}: \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that reduced \langfam{SDD}{T}{s} support negation in polynomial time by passing negation through the subs. The result is still a \langfam{cSDD}{T} formula, hence a \langref{cSDD} formula.

languages.definition: \langref{cSDD} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{cSDD}{T}. \citep{VanDenBroeck_2015,Darwiche_2011}

languages.name: d-DNNF

VA.description: \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Since CT implies VA: A formula is valid iff all $2^n$ assignments satisfy it. If we can count models in polytime (CT), we can check validity by testing if the count equals $2^n$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\VA}.

IM.description:
\supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\VA}. \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CD}. Since VA and CD imply IM: To test whether consistent term $\alpha$ entails representation $\Sigma$, i.e. $\alpha \models \Sigma$, test whether $
eg\alpha \lor \Sigma$ is valid. This is equivalent to $
eg\alpha \lor (\Sigma | \alpha)$. Since $
eg\alpha$ and $\Sigma | \alpha$ share no variables, the disjunction is valid iff $
eg\alpha$ is valid or $\Sigma | \alpha$ is valid. Since $\alpha$ is consistent, $
eg\alpha$ cannot be valid. Thus we test validity of $\Sigma | \alpha$, constructible in polytime via CD and testable in polytime via VA. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}. If \langref{d-DNNF} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\SE}.

EQ.description: \citet{Darwiche_Huang_2002} showed that equivalence of \langref{d-DNNF} representations can be tested in randomized polynomial time by reducing it to the problem of checking whether two \langref{d-DNNF} representations agree on a randomly chosen assignment. Under the derandomization hypothesis P = BPP, this yields a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm and hence \langref{d-DNNF} satisfies EQ.

CT.description: \langref{d-DNNF} satisfies CT: model counting can be performed in polynomial time by exploiting decomposability (multiplying counts at $\land$-nodes) and determinism (adding counts at $\lor$-nodes). This is the key tractability result motivating \langref{d-DNNF} as a compilation target \citep{Darwiche_2001b} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{d-DNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

AND_C.description: Conjunction implies bounded conjunction: bounded conjunction is a special case of conjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under conjunction is also closed under bounded conjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\ANDBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\ANDC}.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, \langref{d-DNNF} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\ORBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\ORC}.

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\FO}.

SFO.description: If \langref{d-DNNF} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{d-DNNF} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{d-DNNF}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both decomposability and determinism. If $\alpha \land \beta \models \bot$ then $(\alpha|\gamma) \land (\beta|\gamma) \models \bot$ for any consistent term $\gamma$, since $((\alpha \land \beta)|\gamma) \land \gamma \models \bot$ and $\gamma$ is consistent \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates; literals as inputs; and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables) and whose OR gates are \emph{deterministic} (on any input, at most one child evaluates to $1$).\citep{Darwiche_2001b}

languages.fullName: Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form
languages.name: d-SDNNF

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uOBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. If \langref{d-SDNNF} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{d-SDNNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{d-SDNNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{d-SDNNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{d-SDNNF} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{d-SDNNF}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{d-SDNNF} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{d-SDNNF} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{d-SDNNF} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{d-SDNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{d-SDNNF} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both structured decomposability and determinism. The vtree structure is maintained since $\text{Vars}(\Sigma|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\Sigma)$, and mutual exclusivity of $\lor$-children is preserved under conditioning \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

FO.description: If \langref{d-SDNNF} satisfied FO, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled to an equivalent \langref{d-SDNNF} of size polynomial in the input \langref{DNF} (using fresh selector variables and applying FO). Since specific \langref{DNF} formulas require exponential-size \langref{d-SDNNF} representations (unconditionally), this is impossible. This unconditional result applies to the fixed-vtree case \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

NOT_C.description: \langref{d-SDNNF} is not closed under negation in polytime. \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} proves that for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, there exists a Boolean function $f$ with an equivalent \langref{d-SDNNF} of size $n$ such that any \langref{SDNNF} equivalent to $\neg f$ has size $n^{\Omega(\tilde{\log}\, n)}$. This super-polynomial lower bound holds for every vtree (not just a single tree), so the result applies to \langref{d-SDNNF} (not just \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}). Whether quasipolynomial closure holds remains open.

SFO.description: \citet[Corollary 1]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives \langref{d-SDNNF} formulas of size $s$ whose existential quantification of one variable requires \langref{d-SDNNF} size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$. Hence \langref{d-SDNNF} does not support singleton forgetting in polynomial time.

languages.definition: \langref{d-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}

languages.fullName: Deterministic Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)
languages.name: d-SDNNF$_T$

EQ.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\SE}. Since SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\CT}.

SE.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\CT}. \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\ANDBC}. Since CT and $\land$BC imply SE: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $|\text{models}(\Sigma_1 \land \Sigma_2)| = |\text{models}(\Sigma_1)|$. Compute the conjunction via $\land$BC, then count models of both the original formula and the conjunction via CT and compare., \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\SE}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\IM}.

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\FO}.

OR_BC.description: \citet[Theorem 5]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations of functions $f$ and $g$ such that, measured in the size $s$ of the corresponding source representations, $f \lor g$ requires \langref{d-SDNNF} size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$. The unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations can be transformed into \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} representations with only polynomial overhead, and every \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} representation can be transformed into \langref{d-SDNNF} with only polynomial overhead. Thus polynomial-time bounded disjunction for \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} would contradict the lower bound.

SFO.description: If \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

NOT_C.description: Negation and bounded conjunction imply bounded disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \lor \gamma = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \land \lnot\gamma)$, where $\lnot\gamma$ (negation of a clause) is a term. Negate $\Sigma$, conjoin with the term $\lnot\gamma$ via bounded conjunction, then negate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\ORBC}. Also, \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\ANDBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\NOTC}.

AND_BC.description: Bounded conjunction of two \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} formulas can be performed in polytime via the Apply algorithm, preserving both determinism and structured decomposability \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves both decomposability and determinism, and it preserves the fixed vtree because restricting by a term only removes variables or replaces literals by constants. Hence the conditioned formula remains in \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

languages.name: DNF
languages.definition:
A \langref{DNF} is a disjunction of terms $\bigvee_{i=1}^mT_i$. A term is a conjunction of literals $T_i=\bigwedge_{j=1}^{k_i}l_j$ where a literal $l_j$ is a variable $x$ or its negation $\lnot x$. For example, $(x \land y \land \lnot z)\lor (x\land \lnot y)\lor (z\land w)$.

Equivalently, a \langref{DNF} is a \langref{DNNF} of depth two with an output OR gate whose children are AND gates over literals.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. If \langref{DNF} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: EQ implies VA: $\mathsf{true}$ and $\mathsf{false}$ belong to every NNF subset. $\Sigma$ is valid iff it is equivalent to $\mathsf{true}$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}.

SE.description: SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\SE}.

IM.description: IM implies VA: A formula $\Sigma$ is valid iff the complete term (all positive or all negative literals, i.e., any single model) implies $\Sigma$. Since IM tests whether a consistent term implies the representation, testing with a trivially consistent term gives validity. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}.

VA.description: Validity checking for \langref{DNF} is coNP-complete. A \langref{DNF} is valid iff every assignment satisfies at least one term, which requires checking that term coverage is complete \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{DNF} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

AND_C.description: In the language \langref{DNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{DNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{DNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{DNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\ORC}. Since Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\ORBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\SFO}.

CD.description: \supportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\ANDBC}. \supportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\SFO}. Since Bounded conjunction and singleton forgetting imply conditioning: $\Sigma|\gamma$ for term $\gamma = l_1 \land \cdots \land l_k$ is computed iteratively by $\Sigma \leftarrow \exists x_i.(\Sigma \land l_i)$ for each literal $l_i$, using $\land$BC then SFO. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\CD}.

AND_BC.description: The conjunction of two \langref{DNF} representations $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ can be computed by taking all cross products of one term from $\alpha_1$ and one from $\alpha_2$, removing redundant literals and inconsistent terms. The result is a \langref{DNF} of polynomial size \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction of \langref{DNF} representations is trivially another \langref{DNF} representation: simply take the union of all terms \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

FO.description: \langref{DNF} satisfies FO \citep{Lang_2003}. Forgetting variables from a \langref{DNF} representation can be done in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.name: DNNF

ME.description: \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\CO}. \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\CD}. Since CO and CD imply ME: First test consistency (polytime via CO). If inconsistent, return the empty set. Otherwise, build a decision tree of models by iterating over variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ and using CD to test consistency of $\Sigma | \alpha \land x_i$ and $\Sigma | \alpha \land \neg x_i$ for each leaf term $\alpha$. Since one branch must succeed for consistent terms, the tree has $O(mn)$ tests where $m$ is the model count and $n$ is the variable count. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\ME}.

CE.description: \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\CO}. \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\CD}. Since CO and CD imply CE: To test whether representation $\Sigma$ entails non-valid clause $\alpha$, i.e. $\Sigma \models \alpha$, it suffices to test whether $\Sigma | \neg\alpha$ is inconsistent. \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\CE}.

VA.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. If \langref{DNNF} supported \VA in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\VA}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. If \langref{DNNF} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\SE}.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. If \langref{DNNF} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. If \langref{DNNF} supported \EQ in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\EQ}.

IM.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. If \langref{DNNF} supported \IM in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\IM}.

CO.description: Consistency of a \langref{DNNF} representation can be decided by a bottom-up traversal. A literal or constant node is handled directly; an $\lor$-node is consistent iff at least one child is consistent; and an $\land$-node is consistent iff all children are consistent. The last test is sound because decomposability ensures that conjuncts mention disjoint variable sets, so independently satisfiable children have compatible models. Hence consistency is decidable in linear time in the circuit size. \citep{Darwiche_2001a,Darwiche_2002}

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{DNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{DNNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{DNNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{DNNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{DNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{DNNF} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{DNNF} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

OR_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\ORC}. Since Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\ORBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\SFO}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction trivially preserves decomposability since decomposability only constrains $\land$-nodes. \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves decomposability: if $\alpha$ and $\beta$ do not share variables, then $\alpha|\gamma$ and $\beta|\gamma$ do not share variables either, since $\text{Vars}(\alpha|\gamma) \subseteq \text{Vars}(\alpha)$ \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

FO.description: \langref{DNNF} satisfies FO: forgetting a set of variables in a \langref{DNNF} representation can be performed in polynomial time while preserving decomposability \citep{Darwiche_2001a} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: Boolean circuits with AND and OR gates, literals as inputs, and whose AND gates are \emph{decomposable} (children mention disjoint sets of variables).\citep{Darwiche_2001a} Decomposability may be seen as the analogue in Boolean circuits to the notion of \emph{syntactic multilinearity} in arithmetic circuits.\citep{shpilka2010arithmetic}

languages.name: FBDD

ME.description: \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\CO}. \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\CD}. Since CO and CD imply ME: First test consistency (polytime via CO). If inconsistent, return the empty set. Otherwise, build a decision tree of models by iterating over variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ and using CD to test consistency of $\Sigma | \alpha \land x_i$ and $\Sigma | \alpha \land \neg x_i$ for each leaf term $\alpha$. Since one branch must succeed for consistent terms, the tree has $O(mn)$ tests where $m$ is the model count and $n$ is the variable count. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\ME}.

CE.description: \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\CO}. \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\CD}. Since CO and CD imply CE: To test whether representation $\Sigma$ entails non-valid clause $\alpha$, i.e. $\Sigma \models \alpha$, it suffices to test whether $\Sigma | \neg\alpha$ is inconsistent. \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\CE}.

IM.description:
\supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\VA}. \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\CD}. Since VA and CD imply IM: To test whether consistent term $\alpha$ entails representation $\Sigma$, i.e. $\alpha \models \Sigma$, test whether $
eg\alpha \lor \Sigma$ is valid. This is equivalent to $
eg\alpha \lor (\Sigma | \alpha)$. Since $
eg\alpha$ and $\Sigma | \alpha$ share no variables, the disjunction is valid iff $
eg\alpha$ is valid or $\Sigma | \alpha$ is valid. Since $\alpha$ is consistent, $
eg\alpha$ cannot be valid. Thus we test validity of $\Sigma | \alpha$, constructible in polytime via CD and testable in polytime via VA. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}}. If \langref{FBDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\SE}.

CO.description: Consistency means existence of a satisfying root-to-1 path. For a general \langref{FBDD}, run graph reachability from the root to terminal 1 in linear time \citep{Gergov_1994,Bryant_1992}.

CT.description: Model counting is done by dynamic programming over the DAG representation of the free BDD, combining child counts at each decision node; total runtime is polynomial in graph size \citep{Gergov_1994,Bryant_1992}.

VA.description: Validity is equivalent to absence of any falsifying path. For a general \langref{FBDD}, check that terminal 0 is unreachable from the root (equivalently, all root-to-terminal paths evaluate to true); this is graph reachability in polynomial time \citep{Gergov_1994,Bryant_1992}.

EQ.description: \citep{Blum_1980} evaluate both free Boolean graphs under random assignments over a finite field; if values differ, the graphs are definitely distinct, and if values match, they are probably equivalent. Repetition decreases error probability exponentially. Under derandomization assumptions such as P = BPP, this also yields deterministic polynomial-time equivalence testing for \langref{FBDD}.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{FBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{FBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{FBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{FBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{FBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{FBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{FBDD}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{FBDD} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{FBDD} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{FBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{FBDD} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, \langref{FBDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\FO}.

NOT_C.description: Negation of an \langref{FBDD} is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The decision property and read-once property are preserved \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the free read-once property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Let $ROW_n$ and $COL_n$ test whether an $n \times n$ Boolean matrix has an all-1 row or an all-1 column. The selector construction $(s \land ROW_n) \lor (\neg s \land COL_n)$ has polynomial-size \langref{FBDD}{s}: the top variable $s$ chooses a rowwise or columnwise computation. Forgetting $s$ yields $ROW_n \lor COL_n$, whose \langref{FBDD} size is $\Omega(n^{-7/2}2^n)$ by \citet[Theorem 6.2.13]{Wegener_2000}.

languages.definition: Binary decision diagrams such that each root-to-sink path tests each variable at most once. Also known as read-once branching programs.\citep{Wegener_2000}

languages.name: IP

EQ.description: \supportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\SE}. Since SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\EQ}.

VA.description: \supportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\IM}. Since IM implies VA: A formula $\Sigma$ is valid iff the complete term (all positive or all negative literals, i.e., any single model) implies $\Sigma$. Since IM tests whether a consistent term implies the representation, testing with a trivially consistent term gives validity. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\VA}.

CT.description: By duality with \langref{PI}: the negation of a formula in prime implicate form is in prime implicant (\langref{IP}) form. The number of models of $\neg\Sigma$ over $\text{Vars}(\Sigma)$ is $2^n$ minus the model count of $\Sigma$. Hence the \#P-hardness of counting for \langref{PI} transfers to \langref{IP} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

IM.description: By definition, \langref{IP} is the set of prime implicants of a formula. A consistent term $\alpha$ implies $\Sigma$ iff $\alpha$ entails some prime implicant of $\Sigma$. This entailment check between terms runs in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: \langref{IP} is a subset of \langref{DNF} and \langref{IP} satisfies IM, so checking sentential entailment reduces to checking implication between prime implicant forms. Two formulas are equivalent iff they share the same prime implicants (canonical form), giving SE \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\ORBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\ORC}.

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\FO}.

NOT_C.description: Dual of \langref{PI}: $\Sigma_n = \bigvee_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \land x_{2i+1})$ is in \langref{IP} form but has $2^n$ prime implicates. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicants. Hence \langref{IP} cannot satisfy $\neg$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: $\text{IP}(\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2) = \max(\{\beta_1 \land \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{IP}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (dual of Proposition 40, \citet{Marquis_2000}). The maximization removes subsuming terms, yielding an \langref{IP} form in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

AND_C.description: It is straightforward to convert a clause into an equivalent \langref{IP} formula in polynomial time. Since specific \langref{CNF} formulas cannot be turned into equivalent \langref{IP} formulas in polynomial space (\langref{CNF} $\not\leq$ \langref{IP}), \langref{IP} does not satisfy $\land$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Combining prime implicants via disjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing terms, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicants. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicant) and $\alpha_2 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k \bigvee_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \land q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicants). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants, an exponential blowup \citep{Chandra_1978} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Given an \langref{IP} formula $\Sigma = \bigvee \gamma_i$, conditioning on $\gamma$ gives $\bigvee (\gamma_i|\gamma)$. Keeping only the logically weakest surviving terms yields a prime implicants formula equivalent to $\Sigma|\gamma$. This requires $O(n^2)$ entailment tests among terms, each polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: The number of prime implicants of $\exists x.\Sigma$ can be exponentially greater than the number of prime implicants of $\Sigma$ \citep{Chandra_1978}. A specific construction with $mk+1$ prime implicants produces $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicants after forgetting one variable \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: A \langref{DNF} that is the disjunction of all minimal entailing terms (prime implicants). \citep{Quine_1952}

languages.name: MODS

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{MODS}}{\langref{CNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\IM}.

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{MODS}}{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}, and \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\EQ}.

SE.description: \compilespoly{\langref{MODS}}{\langref{IP}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\SE}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\SE}.

CO.description: A representation $\Sigma$ is consistent iff it has at least one model. Given the set of models, we need only check nonemptiness of the set \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: We need only take the cardinality of the set of models (which can be done in linear time) \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\SFO}.

CD.description: \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\ANDBC}. \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\SFO}. Since Bounded conjunction and singleton forgetting imply conditioning: $\Sigma|\gamma$ for term $\gamma = l_1 \land \cdots \land l_k$ is computed iteratively by $\Sigma \leftarrow \exists x_i.(\Sigma \land l_i)$ for each literal $l_i$, using $\land$BC then SFO. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\CD}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\ORBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{MODS}}{\ORC}.

NOT_C.description: $\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ has only one model over $\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}$ but its negation $\neg\Sigma$ has $2^n - 1$ models. Hence \langref{MODS} cannot satisfy $\neg$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: The bounded conjunction of two \langref{MODS} formulas (over the same variables) is simply taking the intersection of their model sets. If the variables differ, it is the intersection of their cylindrical extensions. The result is a \langref{MODS} representation in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_C.description: Let $\Sigma_i = (x_{i,1} \lor x_{i,2})$ for $i = 1,\ldots,n$. Each $\Sigma_i$ has 3 models. But $\Sigma = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n \Sigma_i$ has $3^n$ models, so no polynomial-size \langref{MODS} representation exists \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: Let $\alpha_1 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^n x_i$ (1 model) and $\alpha_2 = y$ (1 model). Then $\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2$ has $2^n + 1$ models over $\text{Vars}(\alpha_1) \cup \text{Vars}(\alpha_2)$. Since this already uses the disjunction of just two \langref{MODS} representations, \langref{MODS} does not satisfy $\lor$BC \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

FO.description: Given a \langref{MODS} formula $\Sigma$ and a set $X$, the \langref{MODS} representation of $\exists X.\Sigma$ is obtained by removing every leaf node labeled by a literal $x$ or $\neg x$ with $x \in X$ (Propositions 18 and 20, Lang et al. 2000). See also the polytime FO operation on \langref{DNNF} from \citep{Darwiche_2001a} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition:
The set of satisfying assignments (models) of the function.\citep{Darwiche_2002}

Equivalently, a suitably restricted \langref{DNF} whose terms are in one-to-one correspondence with the models.

languages.name: nFBDD

VA.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\langref{nFBDD}}. If \langref{nFBDD} supported \VA in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\VA}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{uFBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\langref{nFBDD}}. If \langref{nFBDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\SE}.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\langref{nFBDD}}. If \langref{nFBDD} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{nFBDD}}. If \langref{nFBDD} supported \EQ in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\EQ}.

IM.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{nFBDD}}. If \langref{nFBDD} supported \IM in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\IM}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{nFBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{nFBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{nFBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{nFBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{nFBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{nFBDD} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{nFBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{nFBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{nFBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{nFBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{nFBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\ORC}. Since Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\ORBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{nFBDD}}{\SFO}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction of \langref{nFBDD} formulas is obtained by adding a top-level nondeterministic $\lor$-node whose children are the input formulas. This preserves the free read-once property. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the free read-once property. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

FO.description: To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the free read-once property and computes existential quantification. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

languages.definition:
An \langref{nFBDD} is a \emph{nondeterministic} \langref{FBDD}, i.e., an \langref{FBDD} that is additionally allowed to contain \emph{guess} nodes.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Also known as a nondeterministic read-once branching program.

languages.name: NNF

CO.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CO}, and \compilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \CO in polynomial time, then \langref{CNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\CO}.

VA.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \VA in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\VA}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\SE}.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\EQ}, and \compilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \EQ in polynomial time, then \langref{CNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\EQ}.

IM.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \IM in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\IM}.

CE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\CE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \CE in polynomial time, then \langref{CNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\CE}.

ME.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\ME}, and \compilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{NNF}}. If \langref{NNF} supported \ME in polynomial time, then \langref{CNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ME}.

FO.description: If \langref{NNF} supported FO, then the consistency of any \langref{CNF} representation $\Sigma$ could be tested in polynomial time: represent $\Sigma$ in \langref{NNF}, compute $\exists\text{Vars}(\Sigma).\Sigma$, and check the resulting constant representation. This would decide \langref{CNF} consistency in polynomial time, implying P = NP \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ANDC}. Since Conjunction implies bounded conjunction: bounded conjunction is a special case of conjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under conjunction is also closed under bounded conjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ANDBC}.

OR_C.description: \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\NOTC}. \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ANDC}. Since Negation and conjunction imply disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \lor \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, conjoin, then negate the result. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ORC}.

OR_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ORC}. Since Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ORBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\CD}. \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\ORBC}. Since Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component). \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{NNF}}{\SFO}.

NOT_C.description: \langref{NNF} trivially satisfies $\neg$C since any propositional representation can be converted to \langref{NNF} by pushing negations to literals and this preserves the \langref{NNF} property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_C.description: Taking the conjunction of \langref{NNF} representations $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2 \land \ldots$ trivially produces another \langref{NNF} representation, since \langref{NNF} places no restrictions on $\land$-nodes \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: CD is trivially satisfied by \langref{NNF}: replacing literals of the conditioning term by Boolean constants preserves the \langref{NNF} property \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.definition: A Boolean circuit with AND and OR gates with literals as inputs (i.e., negations may only appear at leaves). Also called \emph{De Morgan circuits}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

languages.name: nOBDD

VA.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. If \langref{nOBDD} supported \VA in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\VA}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uOBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. If \langref{nOBDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\SE}.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. If \langref{nOBDD} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. If \langref{nOBDD} supported \EQ in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\EQ}.

IM.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. If \langref{nOBDD} supported \IM in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\IM}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{nOBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{nOBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{nOBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{nOBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{nOBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{nOBDD} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{nOBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{nOBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{nOBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{nOBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{nOBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{nOBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{nOBDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{nOBDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\ORBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\ORC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\SFO}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the ordered decision-diagram property. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

FO.description: To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the ordered decision-diagram property and computes existential quantification. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

languages.definition: \langref{nOBDD} $= \bigcup_{<}$ \langfam{nOBDD}{<}. \citep{Wegener_2000}

languages.fullName: Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)
languages.name: OBDD$_<$

CE.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\CO}. \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\CD}. Since CO and CD imply CE: To test whether representation $\Sigma$ entails non-valid clause $\alpha$, i.e. $\Sigma \models \alpha$, it suffices to test whether $\Sigma | \neg\alpha$ is inconsistent. \citep{Darwiche_2002,Darwiche_2001a}, \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\CE}.

IM.description:
\supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\VA}. \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\CD}. Since VA and CD imply IM: To test whether consistent term $\alpha$ entails representation $\Sigma$, i.e. $\alpha \models \Sigma$, test whether $
eg\alpha \lor \Sigma$ is valid. This is equivalent to $
eg\alpha \lor (\Sigma | \alpha)$. Since $
eg\alpha$ and $\Sigma | \alpha$ share no variables, the disjunction is valid iff $
eg\alpha$ is valid or $\Sigma | \alpha$ is valid. Since $\alpha$ is consistent, $
eg\alpha$ cannot be valid. Thus we test validity of $\Sigma | \alpha$, constructible in polytime via CD and testable in polytime via VA. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\NOTC}. \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\ANDBC}. \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\CO}. Since Negation, bounded conjunction, and consistency imply sentential entailment: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2$ is unsatisfiable. Negate $\Sigma_2$ via $\lnot$C, conjoin with $\Sigma_1$ via $\land$BC, then test consistency via CO. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\SE}.

CO.description: Consistency can be checked in polynomial time by graph reachability: verify that there exists a path from the root to a terminal 1 node \citep{Bryant_1986}.

CT.description: Model counting on \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} can be done in polynomial time by a single bottom-up pass \citep{Bryant_1986, Bryant_1992}.

EQ.description: After reducing both diagrams, \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} are canonical: two functions are equivalent iff their reduced \langfam{OBDD}{<} diagrams are identical. This makes EQ a structural isomorphism check \citep{Bryant_1986, Bryant_1992, Meinel_Theobald_1998} (Theorem 8.11).

ME.description: Models of an \langfam{OBDD}{<} formula can be enumerated in time polynomial in the output size by traversing all root-to-1-sink paths. Each path corresponds to a (partial) assignment that can be expanded to full models. \citep{Bryant_1986}

VA.description: Validity can be checked in polynomial time by graph reachability: verify that no path from the root reaches the terminal 0 node, equivalently that every reachable terminal is 1 \citep{Bryant_1986}.

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{OBDD}{<}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{OBDD}{<} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{OBDD}{<} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{OBDD}{<}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

FO.description: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \langfam{OBDD}{<} would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \langfam{OBDD}{<} representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{OBDD}{<}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\CD}. \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\ORBC}. Since Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component). \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\SFO}.

AND_C.description: Negation and conjunction imply disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \lor \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, conjoin, then negate the result. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\ORC}. Also, \supportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\NOTC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{OBDD}{<}}{\ANDC}.

NOT_C.description: Negation of an \langfam{OBDD}{<} is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The result is an \langfam{OBDD}{<} of identical size \citep{Bryant_1986}.

AND_BC.description: The Apply algorithm computes the conjunction of two \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced \langfam{OBDD}{<} \citep{Bryant_1986}.

OR_BC.description: The Apply algorithm computes the disjunction of two \langfam{OBDD}{<} representations sharing the same variable ordering in polynomial time. The result is a reduced \langfam{OBDD}{<} \citep{Bryant_1986}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on \langfam{OBDD}{<}: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. The fixed order $<$ is preserved \citep{Bryant_1986}.

languages.name: PI

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{PI}}{\langref{CNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{CNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\IM}.

EQ.description: \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\SE}. Since SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\EQ}.

CO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\CE}. Since CE implies CO: A formula $\Sigma$ is inconsistent iff $\Sigma \models \bot$, which is equivalent to $\Sigma$ entailing the empty clause. Clause entailment (CE) can test this directly. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\CO}.

ME.description: \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\CO}. \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\CD}. Since CO and CD imply ME: First test consistency (polytime via CO). If inconsistent, return the empty set. Otherwise, build a decision tree of models by iterating over variables $x_1, \ldots, x_n$ and using CD to test consistency of $\Sigma | \alpha \land x_i$ and $\Sigma | \alpha \land \neg x_i$ for each leaf term $\alpha$. Since one branch must succeed for consistent terms, the tree has $O(mn)$ tests where $m$ is the model count and $n$ is the variable count. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\ME}.

CE.description: By definition, \langref{PI} is the set of prime implicates of a formula. A clause $\gamma$ is entailed by $\Sigma$ iff $\gamma$ subsumes (is a weakening of) some prime implicate of $\Sigma$. This subsumption check runs in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CT.description: The model counting problem for monotone Krom formulas (conjunctions of clauses with at most two positive literals) is \#P-complete \citep{Roth_1996}. Such formulas can be converted to prime implicates form in polynomial time, so \langref{PI} does not satisfy CT \citep{Darwiche_2002}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

SE.description: \langref{PI} is a subset of \langref{CNF} and \langref{PI} satisfies CE, so checking $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ for \langref{CNF} $\Sigma_2$ reduces to checking each clause of $\Sigma_2$ against the prime implicates of $\Sigma_1$. Two formulas are equivalent iff they share the same prime implicates (canonical form), giving SE \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\SFO}.

AND_C.description: Conjunction implies bounded conjunction: bounded conjunction is a special case of conjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under conjunction is also closed under bounded conjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\ANDBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{PI}}{\ANDC}.

NOT_C.description: The formula $\Sigma_n = \bigwedge_{i=0}^{n-1}(x_{2i} \lor x_{2i+1})$ is in \langref{PI} form but has $2^n$ prime implicants. Its negation has $2^n$ prime implicates. Hence \langref{PI} cannot satisfy $\neg$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description: Combining prime implicates via conjunction requires distributing and re-minimizing clauses, which can lead to an exponential explosion of new prime implicates. Let $\alpha_1 = \bigvee_{i=1}^k p_i$ (one prime implicate) and $\alpha_2 = \bigwedge_{i=1}^k \bigwedge_{j=1}^m (\neg p_i \lor q_{i,j})$ ($mk$ prime implicates). Then $\alpha_1 \land \alpha_2$ has $(m+1)^k + mk$ prime implicates, an exponential blowup \citep{Chandra_1978} \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

OR_BC.description: $\text{PI}(\alpha_1 \lor \alpha_2) = \min(\{\beta_1 \lor \beta_2 \mid \beta_1 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_1), \beta_2 \in \text{PI}(\alpha_2)\}, \models)$ (Proposition 40, \citet{Marquis_2000}). The minimization removes subsumed clauses, yielding a \langref{PI} form in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

OR_C.description: It is straightforward to convert any term into an equivalent \langref{PI} formula in polynomial time. Since specific \langref{DNF} formulas cannot be turned into equivalent \langref{PI} formulas in polynomial space (\langref{DNF} $\not\leq$ \langref{PI}), \langref{PI} does not satisfy $\lor$C \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: The prime implicates of $\Sigma \land \gamma$ can be computed in polytime when $\Sigma$ is in \langref{PI} form and $\gamma$ is a term (Proposition 36 and the following paragraph, \citet{Marquis_2000}). Since \langref{PI} satisfies FO, the prime implicates of $\Sigma|\gamma \equiv \exists\text{Vars}(\gamma).(\Sigma \land \gamma)$ follow in polytime \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

FO.description: The prime implicates of $\exists X.\Sigma$ are exactly the prime implicates of $\Sigma$ that do not contain any variable from $X$ (Proposition 55, \citet{Marquis_2000}). Hence FO for \langref{PI} simply filters out clauses containing forgotten variables \citep{Darwiche_2002,Marquis_2000}.

languages.definition: The subset of \langref{CNF} where formulas are the conjunction of all minimal entailed clauses (prime implicates). \citep{Quine_1952}.

languages.name: SDD

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{SDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{SDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \supportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\NOTC}. \supportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\CE}. Since Negation and clausal entailment imply implicant testing: term $\alpha$ implies $\Sigma$ iff $\lnot\Sigma$ entails clause $\lnot\alpha$. Negate $\Sigma$ via $\lnot$C, then test clausal entailment of $\lnot\alpha$ (a clause) via CE. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{cSDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\langref{SDD}}. If \langref{SDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{SDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{SDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{SDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{SDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{SDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description: Negation and disjunction imply conjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma_1 \land \Sigma_2 = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma_1 \lor \lnot\Sigma_2)$. Negate both operands, disjoin, then negate the result. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\ANDC}. Also, \supportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\NOTC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\ORC}.

AND_BC.description:
