There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{SDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{SDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{SDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{SDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{SDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{SDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

SFO.description: If \langref{SDD} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{SDD} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{SDD}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\FO}.

NOT_C.description: An \langref{SDD} can be negated in polytime by applying exclusive-or with $\top$ using the Apply algorithm \citep{Darwiche_2011}.

CD.description: Conditioning an \langref{SDD} $f$ on a literal $l$ is equivalent to computing $f \land l$. Since any literal is a trivially small \langref{SDD} and \langref{SDD} supports $\land$BC in polytime via Apply, conditioning is polytime \citep{Darwiche_2011}.

languages.definition: \langref{SDD} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{SDD}{T}. \citep{Darwiche_2011}

languages.fullName: Sentential Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)
languages.name: SDD$_T$

EQ.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\SE}. Since SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\langref{SDD}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{SDD}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\NOTC}. \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\ANDBC}. \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\CO}. Since Negation, bounded conjunction, and consistency imply sentential entailment: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $\Sigma_1 \land \lnot\Sigma_2$ is unsatisfiable. Negate $\Sigma_2$ via $\lnot$C, conjoin with $\Sigma_1$ via $\land$BC, then test consistency via CO. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{SDD}{T}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{SDD}{T} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{SDD}{T} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{SDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{SDD}{T}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{SDD}{T} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{SDD}{T} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{SDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

FO.description: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \langfam{SDD}{T} would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \langfam{SDD}{T} representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{SDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\CD}. \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\ORBC}. Since Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component). \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\SFO}.

NOT_C.description: Negation of an \langfam{SDD}{T} can be computed in polytime by applying exclusive-or with $\top$ using Apply. Since $\top$ trivially respects any fixed vtree $T$, the result remains an \langfam{SDD}{T} over the same vtree \citep{Darwiche_2011}.

AND_BC.description: The Apply algorithm combines two \langfam{SDD}{T} representations using any Boolean operator in polytime. In particular, two \langfam{SDD}{T}{s} can be conjoined in polytime via Apply \citep{Darwiche_2011}.

OR_BC.description: The Apply algorithm combines two \langfam{SDD}{T} representations using any Boolean operator in polytime. In particular, two \langfam{SDD}{T}{s} can be disjoined in polytime via Apply \citep{Darwiche_2011}.

CD.description: Conditioning an \langfam{SDD}{T} representation $f$ on a literal $l$ is equivalent to computing $f \land l$. Since a literal is a trivially small \langfam{SDD}{T} respecting the same fixed vtree, and \langfam{SDD}{T} supports $\land$BC in polytime via Apply, conditioning is polynomial-time as well \citep{Darwiche_2011}.

languages.definition:
\langfam{SDD}{T} generalizes \langfam{OBDD}{<} by allowing decisions, not only on variables, but on representations (i.e., recursively defined \langref{SDD} formulas).\citep{Darwiche_2011}

Equivalently, an \langfam{SDD}{T} is a \langfam{d-SDNNF}{T} in which determinism is replaced by \emph{strong determinism}, where $\lor$-gates must take the form $ f(x,y) = \bigvee_{i=1}^{m}(p_i(x) \land s_i(y))$, where the $p_i$ are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.

languages.name: SDNNF

VA.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}. If \langref{SDNNF} supported \VA in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\VA}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uOBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{SDNNF}}. If \langref{SDNNF} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\SE}.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langref{nOBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{nOBDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}. If \langref{SDNNF} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{SDNNF}}. If \langref{SDNNF} supported \EQ in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\EQ}.

IM.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{SDNNF}}. If \langref{SDNNF} supported \IM in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\IM}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{SDNNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{SDNNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{SDNNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{SDNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{SDNNF} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langref{SDNNF} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{SDNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{SDNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{SDNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{SDNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{SDNNF} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\ORBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\ORC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langref{SDNNF}}{\SFO}.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves structured decomposability: replacing conditioned literals by constants and simplifying can only remove variables from subformulas, so every decomposable split continues to respect the original vtree. Therefore the result remains an \langref{SDNNF} formula of size polynomial in the input formula \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

FO.description: Forgetting preserves structured decomposability. Replacing all occurrences of literal $x$ and $\neg x$ with $\top$ in an \langref{SDNNF} preserves the vtree structure, since the decomposition at $\land$-nodes remains valid after variable removal. Inherited from \langref{DNNF} \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

languages.definition: \langref{SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}

languages.fullName: Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)
languages.name: SDNNF$_T$

VA.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}. If \langfam{SDNNF}{T} supported \VA in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\VA}.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}. If \langfam{SDNNF}{T} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}. If \langfam{SDNNF}{T} supported \EQ in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\EQ}.

SE.description: SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\EQ}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\SE}.

IM.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}. If \langfam{SDNNF}{T} supported \IM in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\IM}.

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{SDNNF}{T}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{SDNNF}{T} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{SDNNF}{T} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langfam{SDNNF}{T} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}} and \compilesquasi{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

OR_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\ORC}. Since Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\ORBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{SDNNF}{T}}{\SFO}.

AND_BC.description: Bounded conjunction of two \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas can be performed in polytime via the Apply algorithm. The fixed vtree governs variable partitioning at $\land$-nodes, enabling an efficient recursive traversal \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction of \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas preserves structured decomposability, since decomposability only constrains $\land$-nodes and a top-level $\lor$-node introduces no new $\land$-node constraints \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

CD.description: Conditioning preserves decomposability with respect to the same fixed vtree: after replacing conditioned literals by constants, each remaining subformula mentions a subset of its previous variables, so every $\land$-node still decomposes according to the original vtree split \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

FO.description: Darwiche's \langref{DNNF} projection operation for forgetting variables preserves decomposability with respect to any fixed vtree. Applying that operation to a \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formula therefore yields an equivalent \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formula in polynomial time \citet[Proposition 4]{Pipatsrisawat_2008}.

languages.definition:
For each fixed vtree $T$, an \langfam{SDNNF}{T} is a \langref{DNNF} that is additionally structured decomposable, respecting the vtree $T$.\citep{Pipatsrisawat_2008}

Intuitively, all AND gates over the same set of variables must decompose via the same partition, as specified by the vtree.

languages.name: uFBDD

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{uFBDD}}. If \langref{uFBDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{uFBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{uFBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{uFBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{uFBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{uFBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uFBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{uFBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{uFBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uFBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{uFBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, \langref{uFBDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

OR_C.description: Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\ORBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\ORC}.

SFO.description: If \langref{uFBDD} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{uFBDD} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{uFBDD}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{uFBDD}}{\FO}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the free read-once property and cannot create two accepting paths for a remaining assignment. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

languages.definition: The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langref{nFBDD}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.

languages.name: uOBDD

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uOBDD}}. If \langref{uOBDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\SE}.

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{uOBDD}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{uOBDD} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{uOBDD} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{uOBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{uOBDD} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{uOBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{uOBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{uOBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{uOBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{uOBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uOBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{uOBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{uOBDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{uOBDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{uOBDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{uOBDD}}{\FO}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. This preserves the ordered decision-diagram property and cannot create two accepting paths for a remaining assignment. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Theorem 2]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations whose negations require quasipolynomially larger \langref{d-SDNNF} size, measured against the source representation size. An unambiguous \langref{DNF} can be transformed into a \langref{uOBDD} with only polynomial overhead, and every \langref{uOBDD} can be transformed into \langref{d-SDNNF} with only polynomial overhead. Thus polynomial-time negation for \langref{uOBDD} would contradict the lower bound.

SFO.description: The proof of \citet[Corollary 1]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} shows the intractability of SFO for \langref{d-SDNNF}, but also immediately applies to \langref{uOBDD}.

languages.definition: The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langref{nOBDD}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.

languages.name: OBDD

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{cSDD}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\IM}.

CT.description: Any query concerning \langref{OBDD} is equivalent to the corresponding query concerning \langfam{OBDD}{<} when only one DAG is involved. Since \langfam{OBDD}{<} satisfies CO, VA and CT, so does \langref{OBDD} \citep{Darwiche_2002}. \citep{Bryant_1986}

EQ.description: \citet{Meinel_Theobald_1998} cite the polynomial-time equivalence algorithm of \citet{Fortune_1978} for free single-variable program schemes where one input tests predicates in one fixed order on all executions. An \langref{OBDD} is such a free read-once scheme: along every root-to-sink path, variables are tested according to its associated order. The algorithm compares the two diagrams by recursively comparing residual subprograms. When the ordered input next tests variable $x$, both $x=0$ and $x=1$ cofactors are compared against the corresponding restrictions of the other diagram; terminal disagreements reject, and repeated residual pairs are memoized. The number of residual pairs is polynomial in the input sizes, so equivalence of two \langref{OBDD} representations is decidable in polynomial time \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SE.description: Sentential entailment for \langref{OBDD} is coNP-complete. \citet[Lemma 8.14]{Meinel_Theobald_1998} prove that checking consistency of two \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} using different variable orders is NP-complete. Since \langref{OBDD} supports negation, consistency of $\alpha \land \beta$ is equivalent to $\alpha \not\models \neg\beta$, so polynomial-time SE for arbitrary \langref{OBDD}{s} would imply polynomial-time consistency for such pairs \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{OBDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{OBDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{OBDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{OBDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{OBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{OBDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{OBDD}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{OBDD} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{OBDD} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{OBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{OBDD} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{OBDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

FO.description: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \langref{OBDD} would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \langref{OBDD} representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{OBDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}

NOT_C.description: Negation of an \langref{OBDD} is achieved by switching the labels of the 0-sink and 1-sink. The ordered variable property is preserved since the single ordering is not affected \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

CD.description: Since \langref{OBDD} $\subseteq$ \langref{FBDD} and conditioning preserves the variable ordering, \langref{OBDD} also satisfies CD \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

SFO.description: Since only one \langref{OBDD} representation is considered in the transformation, and \langfam{OBDD}{<} satisfies SFO, \langref{OBDD} also satisfies SFO \citep{Darwiche_2002}.

languages.name: TDD$_T$
languages.fullName: Tree Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed vtree)
languages.definition: For each fixed vtree $T$, a \langfam{TDD}{T} is a deterministic tree decision diagram respecting $T$, as introduced by \citet{Capelli_2026}.

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\langfam{SDD}{T}}, and \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\langfam{SDD}{T}}, and \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\SE}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{TDD}{T}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{TDD}{T} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{TDD}{T} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{TDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{TDD}{T}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{TDD}{T} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{TDD}{T} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{TDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

FO.description: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \langfam{TDD}{T} would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \langfam{TDD}{T} representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{TDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}

OR_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\NOTC}. \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\ANDBC}. Since Negation and bounded conjunction imply bounded disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \lor \gamma = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \land \lnot\gamma)$, where $\lnot\gamma$ (negation of a clause) is a term. Negate $\Sigma$, conjoin with the term $\lnot\gamma$ via bounded conjunction, then negate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\ORBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\CD}. \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\ORBC}. Since Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component). \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{TDD}{T}}{\SFO}.

CD.description: Conditioning a \langfam{TDD}{T} on a literal can be performed in polynomial time while preserving the same vtree, as stated in \citet[Theorem 4]{Capelli_2026}.

AND_BC.description: Bounded conjunction is polynomial-time for \langfam{TDD}{T}{s} respecting the same vtree by the apply construction of \citet[Theorem 4]{Capelli_2026}.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Theorem 4]{Capelli_2026} proves that any \langfam{TDD}{T} can be negated in polynomial time while preserving the same vtree.

languages.name: TDD
languages.definition: \langref{TDD} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{TDD}{T}. \citep{Capelli_2026}

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{TDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{TDD}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{TDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{TDD}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{TDD}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{TDD}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{TDD}}. If \langref{TDD} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{TDD}}{\SE}.

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{TDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{TDD}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{TDD} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{TDD}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{TDD}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{TDD} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{TDD}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{TDD} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{TDD} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{TDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{TDD} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{TDD}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{TDD} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{TDD} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{TDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{TDD} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

FO.description: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \langref{TDD} would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \langref{TDD} representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{TDD}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}

CD.description: \citet[Theorem 4]{Capelli_2026} proves that any \langref{TDD} can be conditioned in polynomial time.

SFO.description: \citet[Theorem 4]{Capelli_2026} proves that any \langref{TDD} can be existentially quantified on a single variable in polynomial time.

NOT_C.description: \citet[Theorem 4]{Capelli_2026} proves that any \langref{TDD} can be negated in polynomial time.

languages.name: dec-DNNF

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{FBDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{FBDD}}{\langref{dec-DNNF}}. If \langref{dec-DNNF} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description:
In the language \langref{dec-DNNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{dec-DNNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{dec-DNNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{dec-DNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{dec-DNNF} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{dec-DNNF}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{dec-DNNF} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{dec-DNNF} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{dec-DNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{dec-DNNF} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{dec-DNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{dec-DNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{d-DNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{dec-DNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, \langref{dec-DNNF} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

Note that \citet[Theorem 9]{Bova_2016} proves the lower bound without explicitly noting the implication for Bounded Disjunction.

SFO.description: If \langref{dec-DNNF} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{dec-DNNF} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{dec-DNNF}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{dec-DNNF}}{\FO}.

CD.description: A \langref{dec-DNNF} can be viewed as a decision diagram with additional decomposable $\land$-nodes. Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. The extra $\land$-nodes cause no problem: recurse through them, and decomposability is preserved because conditioning only removes variables from subcircuits. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

languages.name: dec-SDNNF
languages.fullName: Decision Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\SE}, and \compilespoly{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}. If \langref{dec-SDNNF} supported \SE in polynomial time, then \langref{OBDD} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\SE}.

OR_C.description:
In the language \langref{dec-SDNNF}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{dec-SDNNF} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langref{dec-SDNNF} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langref{dec-SDNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{dec-SDNNF} does not support Bounded Disjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Disjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

In the language \langref{dec-SDNNF}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langref{dec-SDNNF} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langref{dec-SDNNF} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langref{dec-SDNNF}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

Alternatively, since \langref{dec-SDNNF} does not support Bounded Conjunction in quasipolynomial time, intractability of Conjunction is immediate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{DNNF} computing $f\land g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{dec-SDNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\langref{DNNF}}, \langref{dec-SDNNF} cannot support bounded conjunction.

OR_BC.description:
There are Boolean functions $f,g:\{0,1\}^n\to\{0,1\}$, each computable by \langref{OBDD}{s} of size $O(n)$, such that any \langref{SDNNF} computing $f\lor g$ has size $2^{\Omega(\sqrt{n})}$ \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}.

As a result, because \compilesquasi{\langref{OBDD}}{\langref{dec-SDNNF}} and \compilesquasi{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\langref{SDNNF}}, \langref{dec-SDNNF} cannot support bounded disjunction in quasipolynomial time.

SFO.description: If \langref{dec-SDNNF} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langref{dec-SDNNF} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langref{dec-SDNNF}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langref{dec-SDNNF}}{\FO}.

CD.description: A \langref{dec-SDNNF} can be viewed as a decision diagram with additional structured decomposable $\land$-nodes. Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. The extra $\land$-nodes cause no problem: recurse through them, and structured decomposability is preserved because conditioning only removes variables from subcircuits. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

languages.definition: \langref{dec-SDNNF} $= \bigcup_T$ \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}. \citep{Amarilli_2018}

languages.name: dec-SDNNF$_T$
languages.fullName: Decision Structured Decomposable Negation Normal Form (wrt a fixed vtree)

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}, and \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\CT}.

SE.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}, and \supportspoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\SE}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\SE}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\IM}.

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

SFO.description: If \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\FO}.

OR_BC.description: Conditioning and bounded disjunction imply singleton forgetting: to forget variable $x$ from $\Sigma$, compute $\Sigma|_{x=0}$ and $\Sigma|_{x=1}$ via conditioning, then combine via $\Sigma|_{x=0} \lor \Sigma|_{x=1}$ using bounded disjunction (one operand can serve as the bounded component). \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\SFO}. Also, \supportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\CD}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T}}{\ORBC}.

CD.description: For each fixed vtree $T$, a \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} can be viewed as a decision diagram with additional decomposable $\land$-nodes structured by $T$. Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. The extra $\land$-nodes cause no problem: recurse through them, and the fixed vtree is preserved because conditioning only removes variables from subcircuits. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

languages.definition:
For each fixed vtree $T$, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} is the subset of \langref{dec-DNNF} that respects $T$.\citep{darwiche2007language,Amarilli_2018}

Equivalently, \langfam{dec-SDNNF}{T} formulas are \langfam{SDNNF}{T} formulas whose OR-gates may only appear in \emph{decisions/tests}.

languages.name: cSDD$_T$
languages.fullName: Compressed Sentential Decision Diagrams (wrt a fixed vtree)

EQ.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\langfam{SDD}{T}}, and \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\EQ}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\langref{cSDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\langref{SDD}}. \compilespoly{\langref{SDD}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\CT}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\langref{cSDD}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{cSDD}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\IM}.

SE.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\langfam{SDD}{T}}, and \supportspoly{\langfam{SDD}{T}}{\SE}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\SE}.

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{cSDD}{T}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{cSDD}{T} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{cSDD}{T} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{cSDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{cSDD}{T}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{cSDD}{T} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{cSDD}{T} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{cSDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

FO.description: A quasipolynomial-time forgetting procedure for \langfam{cSDD}{T} would turn every \langref{DNF} representation into an equivalent \langfam{cSDD}{T} representation of quasipolynomial size in the input \langref{DNF} after introducing fresh selectors and forgetting them. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{cSDD}{T}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002,VanDenBroeck_2015}

OR_BC.description: Negation and bounded disjunction imply bounded conjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \land \alpha = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \lor \lnot\alpha)$, where $\lnot\alpha$ (negation of a term) is a clause. Negate $\Sigma$, disjoin with the clause $\lnot\alpha$ via bounded disjunction, then negate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\ANDBC}. Also, \supportspoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\NOTC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{cSDD}{T}}{\ORBC}.

NOT_C.description: \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that reduced \langfam{SDD}{T}{s} support negation in polynomial time, by passing negation through the subs.

AND_BC.description: \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that combining two \langfam{cSDD}{T} formulas can force exponential \langfam{cSDD}{T} size.

CD.description: \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that conditioning a \langfam{cSDD}{T} formula on a literal can force exponential \langfam{cSDD}{T} size.

SFO.description: \citet{VanDenBroeck_2015} proved that forgetting one variable from a \langfam{cSDD}{T} formula can force exponential \langfam{cSDD}{T} size.

languages.definition: \langfam{cSDD}{T} is the subset of \langfam{SDD}{T} that is \emph{compressed}, a restriction that makes the representation canonical (i.e., there is a unique representation for every function). \citep{Darwiche_2011}

languages.name: nOBDD$_<$
languages.fullName: Nondeterministic Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)
languages.definition:
An \langfam{nOBDD}{<} is a \emph{nondeterministic} \langfam{OBDD}{<}, i.e., an \langfam{OBDD}{<} that is additionally allowed to contain \emph{guess} nodes.\citep{Wegener_2000}

Also known as a nondeterministic oblivious read-once branching program respecting variable order $<$.

VA.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\VA}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. If \langfam{nOBDD}{<} supported \VA in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\VA}.

CT.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{IP}}{\CT}, and \compilespoly{\langref{IP}}{\langref{DNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. If \langfam{nOBDD}{<} supported \CT in polynomial time, then \langref{IP} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\CT}.
CT.assumption: the polynomial hierarchy does not collapse

EQ.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\EQ}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. If \langfam{nOBDD}{<} supported \EQ in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\EQ}.

SE.description: SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\EQ}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\SE}.

IM.description: \nosupportspoly{\langref{DNF}}{\IM}, and \compilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}. If \langfam{nOBDD}{<} supported \IM in polynomial time, then \langref{DNF} would support it by compiling first. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\IM}.

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{nOBDD}{<}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{nOBDD}{<} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{nOBDD}{<} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

NOT_C.description: \citet[Lemma 5]{Bova_2014} gives 2-\langref{DNF} formulas $D$ whose negations require \langref{DNNF} size $2^{\Omega(\operatorname{size}(D))}$. If \langfam{nOBDD}{<} supported Negation in quasipolynomial time, then \compilesquasi{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}} and \compilesquasi{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\langref{DNNF}} would yield quasipolynomial-size \langref{DNNF} formulas for those negations, contradicting the lower bound.

OR_BC.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\ORC}. Since Disjunction implies bounded disjunction: bounded disjunction is a special case of disjunction where one operand has bounded size, so any language closed under disjunction is also closed under bounded disjunction. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\ORBC}.

SFO.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\FO}. Since Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{nOBDD}{<}}{\SFO}.

OR_C.description: Disjunction of \langfam{nOBDD}{<}{s} is obtained by adding a top-level nondeterministic $\lor$-node whose children are the input formulas. Since all inputs respect the same fixed order $<$, the result is still a \langfam{nOBDD}{<} formula. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

AND_BC.description: The usual product algorithm for conjoining \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} respecting the same variable order works for \langfam{nOBDD}{<}{s}. Run the two diagrams in parallel according to the fixed order $<$, and accept exactly when both components accept. The resulting diagram still respects $<$. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives the analogous construction for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. The fixed order $<$ is preserved. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

FO.description: To forget a variable, replace each decision node labeled by that variable with a nondeterministic $\lor$-node over its two children. Repeating this for all forgotten variables preserves the fixed order $<$ and computes existential quantification. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

languages.name: uOBDD$_<$
languages.fullName: Unambiguous Ordered Binary Decision Diagram (wrt a fixed variable order)
languages.definition: The \emph{unambiguous} variant of \langfam{nOBDD}{<}.\citep{Wegener_2000} That is, on any input there is at most one accepting sequence of guesses.

EQ.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\SE}. Since SE implies EQ: Sentences $\Sigma_1$ and $\Sigma_2$ are equivalent iff $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ and $\Sigma_2 \models \Sigma_1$. \citep{Darwiche_2002}, \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\EQ}.

CT.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}. \compilespoly{\langfam{d-SDNNF}{T}}{\langref{d-SDNNF}}. \compilespoly{\langref{d-SDNNF}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\CT}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\CT}.

SE.description: \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\CT}. \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\ANDBC}. Since CT and $\land$BC imply SE: $\Sigma_1 \models \Sigma_2$ iff $|\text{models}(\Sigma_1 \land \Sigma_2)| = |\text{models}(\Sigma_1)|$. Compute the conjunction via $\land$BC, then count models of both the original formula and the conjunction via CT and compare., \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\SE}.

IM.description: \compilespoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\langref{d-DNNF}}, and \supportspoly{\langref{d-DNNF}}{\IM}. Therefore \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\IM}.

OR_C.description: In the language \langfam{uOBDD}{<}, every term has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{uOBDD}{<} supported $\lor$C, then every \langref{DNF} could be compiled into \langfam{uOBDD}{<} by disjoining the translated terms. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{DNF}}{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

AND_C.description: In the language \langfam{uOBDD}{<}, every clause has a polynomial-size representation. If \langfam{uOBDD}{<} supported $\land$C, then every \langref{CNF} could be compiled into \langfam{uOBDD}{<} by conjoining the translated clauses. This contradicts \nocompilespoly{\langref{CNF}}{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}} \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}. \citep{Darwiche_2002}

OR_BC.description: \citet[Theorem 5]{Vinall-Smeeth_2024} gives unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations of functions $f$ and $g$ such that, measured in the size $s$ of the corresponding source representations, $f \lor g$ requires \langref{d-SDNNF} size $s^{\widetilde{\Omega}(\log s)}$. The unambiguous \langref{DNF} representations can be transformed into \langfam{uOBDD}{<} representations with only polynomial overhead, and every \langfam{uOBDD}{<} representation can be transformed into \langref{d-SDNNF} with only polynomial overhead. Thus polynomial-time bounded disjunction for \langfam{uOBDD}{<} would contradict the lower bound.

SFO.description: If \langfam{uOBDD}{<} supported Singleton Forgetting in polynomial time, then given two \langfam{uOBDD}{<} circuits $f$ and $g$ we could form the decision $(x \land f) \lor (\neg x \land g)$ with only constant overhead and forget $x$, yielding $f \lor g$. This would give polynomial-time Bounded Disjunction for \langfam{uOBDD}{<}, contradicting the lower bound of \citet{Vinall-Smeeth_2024}. \citep{TCZ_Forthcoming}

FO.description: Forgetting implies singleton forgetting: singleton forgetting eliminates a single variable, which is a special case of forgetting an arbitrary set of variables. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\SFO}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\FO}.

NOT_C.description: Negation and bounded conjunction imply bounded disjunction (De Morgan): $\Sigma \lor \gamma = \lnot(\lnot\Sigma \land \lnot\gamma)$, where $\lnot\gamma$ (negation of a clause) is a term. Negate $\Sigma$, conjoin with the term $\lnot\gamma$ via bounded conjunction, then negate. \citep{Darwiche_2002}. However, \nosupportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\ORBC}. Also, \supportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\ANDBC}. Therefore \nosupportspoly{\langfam{uOBDD}{<}}{\NOTC}.

CD.description: Conditioning is easy on branching programs: replace each decision node labeled by a conditioned variable with the child consistent with the conditioning term. The fixed order $<$ is preserved, and the operation cannot create two accepting paths for a remaining assignment. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives this argument for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

AND_BC.description: The usual product algorithm for conjoining \langfam{OBDD}{<}{s} respecting the same variable order works for \langfam{uOBDD}{<}{s}. Run the two diagrams in parallel according to the fixed order $<$, and accept exactly when both components accept. The result is still unambiguous: for any satisfying assignment, each input has at most one accepting path, so the product has at most one accepting path. \citet{Wegener_2000} gives the analogous construction for EXOR-OBDDs in Theorem 10.5.1.

references.title: SDDs Are Exponentially More Succinct than OBDDs
references.bibtex: @article{Bova_2016_a, title={SDDs Are Exponentially More Succinct than OBDDs}, volume={30}, url={https://ojs.aaai.org/index.php/AAAI/article/view/10107}, DOI={10.1609/aaai.v30i1.10107}, abstractNote={ <p> Introduced by Darwiche (2011), sentential decision diagrams (SDDs) are essentially as tractable as ordered binary decision diagrams (OBDDs), but tend to be more succinct in practice. This makes SDDs a prominent representation language, with many applications in artificial intelligence and knowledge compilation. We prove that SDDs are more succinct than OBDDs also in theory, by constructing a family of boolean functions where each member has polynomial SDD size but exponential OBDD size. This exponential separation improves a quasipolynomial separation recently established by Razgon (2014), and settles an open problem in knowledge compilation (Darwiche, 2011). </p> }, number={1}, journal={Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence}, author={Bova, Simone}, year={2016}, month={Feb.} }

references.title: SDD: a new canonical representation of propositional knowledge bases
references.bibtex:
@inproceedings{Darwiche_2011,
author = {Darwiche, Adnan},
title = {SDD: a new canonical representation of propositional knowledge bases},
year = {2011},
isbn = {9781577355144},
publisher = {AAAI Press},
abstract = {We identify a new representation of propositional knowledge bases, the Sentential Decision Diagram (SDD), which is interesting for a number of reasons. First, it is canonical in the presence of additional properties that resemble reduction rules of OBDDs. Second, SDDs can be combined using any Boolean operator in polytime. Third, CNFs with n variables and treewidth w have canonical SDDs of size O(n2w), which is tighter than the bound on OBDDs based on pathwidth. Finally, every OBDD is an SDD. Hence, working with the latter does not preclude the former.},
booktitle = {Proceedings of the Twenty-Second International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence - Volume Two},
pages = {819--826},
numpages = {8},
location = {Barcelona, Catalonia, Spain},
series = {IJCAI'11},
doi = {10.5591/978-1-57735-516-8/IJCAI11-143}
}

url = {https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.5555/2540128.2540255},
publisher = {AAAI Press},
abstract = {The knowledge compilation map introduced by Darwiche and Marquis takes advantage of a number of concepts (mainly queries, transformations, expressiveness, and succinctness) to compare the relative adequacy of representation languages to some AI problems. However, the framework is limited to the comparison of languages that are interpreted in a homogeneous way (formul\ae{} are interpreted as Boolean functions). This prevents one from comparing, on a formal basis, languages that are close in essence, such as OBDD, MDD, and ADD. To fill the gap, we present a generalized framework into which comparing formally heterogeneous representation languages becomes feasible. In particular, we explain how the key notions of queries and transformations, expressiveness, and succinctness can be lifted to the generalized setting.},
booktitle = {Proceedings of the Twenty-Third International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence},
pages = {877--883},
numpages = {7},
location = {Beijing, China},
series = {IJCAI '13}
}

references.title: Approximation of boolean functions by combinatorial rectangles
references.bibtex:
@article{Sauerhoff_2003,
title = {Approximation of boolean functions by combinatorial rectangles},
journal = {Theoretical Computer Science},
volume = {301},
number = {1},
pages = {45--78},
year = {2003},
issn = {0304-3975},
doi = {10.1016/S0304-3975(02)00568-6},
url = {https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304397502005686},
author = {Martin Sauerhoff},
keywords = {Branching programs, Communication complexity, Lower bounds, Approximation, Nondeterminism, Randomness},
abstract = {This paper deals with the number of monochromatic combinatorial rectangles required to approximate a boolean function on a constant fraction of all inputs, where each rectangle may use its own partition of the input variables. The main result of the paper is that the number of rectangles required for the approximation of boolean functions in this model is very sensitive to the allowed error. There is an explicitly defined sequence of boolean functions fn on n variables such that fn has rectangle approximations with a constant number of rectangles and one-sided error 13+o(1) or two-sided error 14+o(1), but, on the other hand, fn requires exponentially many rectangles if the error bounds are decreased by an arbitrarily small constant. As applications of this result, the following separation results for read-once branching programs are obtained. The functions from the main result require only linear size for nondeterministic read-once branching programs and randomized read-once branching programs with two-sided error 13+o(1), while randomized read-once branching programs with constant two-sided error smaller than 13 and unambiguous nondeterministic read-once branching programs require exponential size.}
}

references.title: A Large Lower Bound for 1-Branching Programs
Knowledge compilation and theory approximation
references.bibtex:
@article{Selman_1996,
author = {Selman, Bart and Kautz, Henry},
title = {Knowledge compilation and theory approximation},
year = {1996},
issue_date = {March 1996},
publisher = {Association for Computing Machinery},
address = {New York, NY, USA},
volume = {43},
number = {2},
issn = {0004-5411},
url = {https://doi.org/10.1145/226643.226644},
doi = {10.1145/226643.226644},
abstract = {Computational efficiency is a central concern in the design of knowledge representation systems. In order to obtain efficient systems, it has been suggested that one should limit the form of the statements in the knowledge base or use an incomplete inference mechanism. The former approach is often too restrictive for practical applications, whereas the latter leads to uncertainty about exactly what can and cannot be inferred from the knowledge base. We present a third alternative, in which knowledge given in a general representation language is translated (compiled) into a tractable form--allowing for efficient subsequent query answering.We show how propositional logical theories can be compiled into Horn theories that approximate the original information. The approximations bound the original theory from below and above in terms of logical strength. The procedures are extended to other tractable languages (for example, binary clauses) and to the first-order case. Finally, we demonstrate the generality of our approach by compiling concept descriptions in a general frame-based language into a tractable form.},
journal = {J. ACM},
month = mar,
pages = {193--224},
numpages = {32},
keywords = {Horn clauses, efficient reasoning methods, knowledge compilation, knowledge-base optimization, query evaluation, theory approximation}
}

CO.label: Consistency

VA.label: Validity

CE.description: Check if a clause is entailed

IM.description: Find an implicant of the formula

EQ.label: Equivalence
EQ.description: Check logical equivalence of two formulas

SE.label: Sentential Entailment
SE.description: Check if one formula entails another

CT.label: Model Counting
CT.description: Count the number of satisfying assignments

CD.label: Conditioning

FO.label: Forgetting

SFO.description: Forget a single variable
